chat.author: Erik Nygren
chat.text: But as a concrete example for Chairs Question 4 ("Should we jump in with DNS as a starting point, or investigate other options before starting protocol work?", if use-cases here want one party to selectively disclose results based on an authenticated client identity to a different unrelated party (eg, using a different resolver) and care about confidentiality of the results, then the answer is NO and DNS is the wrong answer.  And if "construct complex query logic and receive an enumerated list of results" then DNS is also likely the wrong answer.  At least some of the proposed problems fall into one of both of these cases.

chat.author: Jari Arkko
chat.text: @Stephen you asked proof that something new is needed. We did a survey https://datatracker.ietf.org/meeting/126/materials/slides-126-t2trg-a-survey-of-ai-agent-discovery-mechanisms-01 presented in t2trg this week. Clearly, you can already do discovery using existing mechanisms, e.g., API discovery, COAP registries, HTTP caching, etc. But also there are aspects that aren't available today. The crux of this bof is doing something useful  that goes beyond what we already have and use, solve some new things, and maybe provide some interoperability, while not blowing up the charter to do an excessive amount

chat.author: Ramesh Raskar
chat.text:
I stepped away and ask Gemini to summarize the chat so far: 
Ambiguous Problem Statement: Critics, including Eric Rescorla and Martin Thomson, expressed frustration that the problem statement felt abstract and that "AI" was potentially being used as a buzzword for challenges that are already solvable with existing infrastructure.1
The DNS Debate: A major point of contention was the proposed reliance on DNS for discovery. While some saw DNS as a potential bootstrap, others argued that using DNS for complex queries or enumeration is a "terrible approach" and fundamentally ill-suited for the task.1
Scope and Trust Concerns: Participants were alarmed that "trust" and "security" appeared to be treated as out of scope. Many argued that internet-wide discovery is impossible without first solving prerequisite problems like global identity, payment, and reputation/attestation.1
Confusion Over Terminology: The term "MDI" (used to describe minimal discovery metadata) caused repeated confusion, as it lacked a concrete, agreed-upon definition, leading to some frustration among attendees.1
Existing Standards: There was a strong sentiment that existing standards (like APIs or registries) already cover basic discovery, and that any new effort must be narrowly focused to solve specific gaps—such as cryptographic interoperability or semantic search—rather than attempting to standardize the entire stack.1

chat.author: Erik Nygren
chat.text:
I could well see a something concretely structured like this that might be successful:
1) Named authorities for reaching enumeration/discovery services within given contexts (eg, Semantic Web).  
2) Definition of protocols for talking to those services for enumerating entities.  (This seems like something HTTP-based, with lots of options.)
3) Ways of translating from entity URIs to endpoints.  (eg, how to go from the URI for an MCP server or A2A gateway to a service endpoint).  DNS seems just fine for this, although in the p2p/local contexts we don't have good security stories (eg, see SETTLE and "HTTPS for Local Domains")
It seems like the first of these is the hard one (eg, what search/enumeration services do you trust/use).  Perhaps if we scoped it like "I am looking for shoes on example.com" that you can scope this to "how do we find the enumeration service for "example.com".

I think this is why something like this needs to be tiered for Discovery:
1) A way to name and get to an authenticated discovery service.  That service can have a name in the DNS.
2) The discovery service itself.  DNS seems like the wrong tool here given the requirements.
3) A way to go from results (eg, URIs) from the discovery service to entity service endpoints.  (DNS might be fine for the A2T/C2S case, more challenging for the A2A/P2P case absent a gatewway).
Are we thinking DAWN would be this whole framework or just one piece of this?

chat.author: Mingxuan Liu
chat.text:
I support standardizing discovery, but I am concerned about the scope. In particular, if local service discovery is included, it may expose important internal capabilities or services. 
We have actually observed this type of vulnerability in one of our current research studies. With LLMs and agents, the risk may be even higher, because exposed discovery information could be abused to infer or misuse sensitive services.
For that reason, I think trust must be part of the working group scope, including how discovered information is authenticated, authorized, and safely used.
